667	\begin{lemma}\leanok
668	[Inductive-step engine from a degreewise short exact sequence]
669	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}
670	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofDiffEq}
671	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
672	  Let \(h_M, h_C, h_K : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d, N \in \mathbb{N}\).
673	  Suppose \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, d)\)
674	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}), and that for all \(n > N\)
675	  \[
676	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
677	  \]
678	  Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\). This packages the entire power-series
679	  side of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step: \(h_M\) is the Hilbert function of
680	  \(M\), and \(h_C, h_K\) are the Hilbert functions of the cokernel
681	  \(C = M/xM\) and kernel \(K = \ker(x : M \to M(1))\) of multiplication by a
682	  degree-one element \(x\); the displayed identity is the alternating-sum
683	  consequence of the degreewise short exact sequence
684	  \(0 \to K_n \to M_n \xrightarrow{x} M_{n+1} \to C_{n+1} \to 0\). Given the
685	  inductive hypothesis that \(h_C, h_K\) are rational of order \(d\), the
686	  conclusion is rationality of \(h_M\) at order \(d+1\).
687	\end{lemma}
688	
689	\begin{proof}
690	  \uses{lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
691	  Set \(g(n) = h_C(n) - h_K(n-1)\). By closure under right shift
692	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}) \(n \mapsto h_K(n-1)\) is rational of order
693	  \(d\), and by closure under difference (\cref{lem:ratHilb_sub}) so is \(g\). The
694	  hypothesis rewrites as \(h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) = g(n+1)\) for \(n > N\) (since
695	  \(g(n+1) = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)\)). Applying the antidifference step
696	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}) to \(H = h_M\) and this \(g\) gives
697	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\).
698	\end{proof}

700	\begin{theorem}[Hilbert polynomial from the section module]
701	  \label{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
702	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomialOfSectionModule}
703	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
704	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
705	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
706	  % Polynomials" (read from
707	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L478).
708	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
709	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
710	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
711	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
712	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
713	  %  we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$
714	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
715	  %  the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
716	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$."
717	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
718	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
719	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
720	  Then there is a unique polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
721	  such that for all \(m \gg 0\)
722	  \[
723	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
724	    \;=\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr).
725	  \]
726	  This \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is the Hilbert polynomial of
727	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}.
728	\end{theorem}
729	
730	\begin{proof}
731	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
732	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
733	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the section graded module
734	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded module over the
735	  Noetherian graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\), with finite-dimensional components and
736	  degree-zero part the field \(\kappa(s)\). Applying the abstract graded
737	  Hilbert--Serre rationality lemma (\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) to the
738	  graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) and the finitely generated
739	  graded module \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) exhibits the graded Hilbert
740	  function
741	  \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
742	  as the eventual coefficient function of a rational series: there are
743	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N\) with
744	  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
745	  = [X^m]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\) for \(m > N\). Apply
746	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} over the characteristic-zero field
747	  \(F = \mathbb{Q}\) to this pair \((p, d)\): it yields a unique polynomial
748	  \(h \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) whose values \(h(n)\) eventually equal
749	  \([X^n]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\). Setting
750	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} := h\) (with the variable renamed \(\lambda\)) gives a
751	  polynomial with \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = \dim_{\kappa(s)}
752	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) for \(m \gg 0\). Uniqueness
753	  is the \(\exists!\) clause of \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}: any two
754	  polynomials agreeing with the Hilbert function for \(m \gg 0\) agree at
755	  infinitely many points, hence are equal.
756	\end{proof}

758	\subsection{The ambient subquotient induction for the Stacks~00K1 step}
759	\label{subsec:gradedModuleApi}
760	
761	The power-series side of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} is fully isolated
762	in the \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) toolkit (\cref{subsec:isRatHilb}). What remains is
763	the \emph{graded-module} side of the Stacks~00K1 induction. Rather than build the
764	kernel and cokernel of multiplication by a degree-one element as graded modules
765	over a smaller quotient ring \(R/(x)\) -- which would force a graded structure on a
766	derived carrier -- we range the induction over \emph{pairs of homogeneous
767	submodules of a single fixed ambient graded module} \(M\). A pair \((N, N')\) with
768	\(N' \le N\) homogeneous submodules of \(M\) stands for the subquotient \(N/N'\); its
769	ambient Hilbert function is the difference \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap M_n) -
Let me read the Lean source for these declarations and the rest of the chapter section.

770	\dim_\kappa(N' \cap M_n)\). The class of such pairs, equipped with finitely many
771	pairwise-commuting degree-one endomorphisms preserving \(N\) and \(N'\), is
772	\emph{closed} under the kernel and cokernel of one of those endomorphisms, both
773	again presented as ambient subquotient pairs. Consequently no graded quotient ring,
774	graded quotient module, or regrading of a subtype/quotient carrier is ever formed:
775	every object that appears is an ambient family \(n \mapsto N_{\mathrm{aux}} \cap M_n\)
776	of submodules of the fixed \(M\).
777	
778	This subsection records that machinery as a thin layer over existing Mathlib
779	scaffold: Mathlib already provides the notion of a homogeneous submodule
780	(\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}), the homogeneity of a span of
781	homogeneous elements (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}), the degree-shift
782	of multiplication by a homogeneous element
783	(\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}), the rank--nullity identity
784	(\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}), and the finite-generation
785	transfer along a surjection (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}).
786	What is supplied here is the internal direct-sum decomposition of a homogeneous
787	submodule as an ambient family (the split \(G_1\)), the subquotient Hilbert data,
788	the kernel/cokernel closure, the degreewise difference identity, the ambient
789	finiteness transfer, and the induction itself.
790	
791	\emph{Setting for this subsection.} Let \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\) be a graded
792	ring whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a field and which is generated as a
793	\(\kappa\)-algebra in degree one, and let \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) be a graded
794	\(\kappa\)-vector space (the fixed ambient module) with finite-dimensional
795	components \(M_n\). All submodules \(N, N', \dots\) below are homogeneous submodules
796	of \(M\), and the relevant endomorphisms are \(\kappa\)-linear maps \(M \to M\)
797	raising degree by one -- typically multiplication by a homogeneous element
798	\(x \in R_1\).

903	  Let \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\) be an internally graded \(\kappa\)-module and let
904	  \(p \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule

918	  subfamily \(n \mapsto M_n \cap p\) consists of submodules with \(M_n \cap p \le M_n\),

923	\begin{lemma}\leanok
924	[$G_1$ (supremum) -- $\bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p) = p$ for $p$ homogeneous]
925	  \label{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}
926	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_inf_eq}
927	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
928	  Let \(p \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
929	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then
930	  \[
931	    \bigsqcup_{n} (M_n \cap p) \;=\; p .
932	  \]
933	  Together with the independence of
934	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}, this is the ambient internal
935	  direct sum \(p = \bigoplus_n (M_n \cap p)\), stated entirely inside \(M\) without
936	  requiring a direct-sum decomposition of the subtype \(\smash{\hat p}\).
937	\end{lemma}
938	
939	\begin{proof}
940	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
941	  The inclusion \(\bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p) \le p\) is clear, since each
942	  \(M_n \cap p \le p\). For the reverse inclusion, take \(m \in p\) and write
943	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in M_n\) using the
944	  internal decomposition of \(M\). Homogeneity of \(p\) gives
945	  \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p\), hence \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in M_n \cap p\); thus
946	  \(m\) is a finite sum of elements of the \(M_n \cap p\), i.e.
947	  \(m \in \bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p)\).
948	\end{proof}

950	\subsubsection*{The ambient subquotient class and its Hilbert function}
951	
952	\begin{definition}\leanok
953	[Subquotient Hilbert data]
954	  \label{def:graded_subquotientHilb}
955	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotientHilb}
956	  \uses{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
957	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}
958	  Fix a graded \(\kappa\)-module \(\mathcal{M}\), i.e.\ \(M\) together with an
959	  internal decomposition \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) whose components
960	  \(\mathcal{M}_n\) are finite-dimensional over \(\kappa\). A \emph{subquotient
961	  datum of length \(r\)} consists of:
962	  \begin{itemize}
963	    \item a pair of homogeneous submodules \(N' \le N \subseteq M\); and
964	    \item a family \(t : \{0, \dots, r-1\} \to (M \to_\kappa M)\) of pairwise
965	      commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms, each raising degree by one
966	      (\(t_i(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\)), each preserving \(N\)
967	      and \(N'\) (\(t_i N \subseteq N\), \(t_i N' \subseteq N'\)); together with
968	    \item a finiteness condition: \(M\) is a finite module over the polynomial ring
969	      \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) acting through the \(t_i\).
970	      % NOTE: (iter-018) the Lean `SubquotientDatum.hfin` records finiteness of the
971	      % SUBQUOTIENT N/N' (not of M) over MvPolynomial (Fin r) κ — mathematically equivalent
972	      % for the induction and the realized encoding. Planner: align this prose to "N/N' is
973	      % finite over κ[t]" and add \lean{...SubquotientDatum} + \lean{...SubquotientDatum.hilb} pins.
974	  \end{itemize}
975	  The pair \((N, N')\) represents the subquotient \(N/N'\). Its \emph{ambient
976	  Hilbert function} is
977	  \[
978	    \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; \Bigl(\dim_\kappa (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
979	      \;-\; \dim_\kappa (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\Bigr) \;\in\; \mathbb{Q},
980	  \]
981	  the difference of the (finite) \(\kappa\)-dimensions of the ambient intersections,
982	  computed in \(\mathbb{Z}\) and cast to \(\mathbb{Q}\). Since \(N' \le N\) one has
983	  \(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n \le N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), so
984	  \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = \dim_\kappa\bigl((N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\bigr)\)
985	  is the dimension of the degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\). The split \(G_1\)
986	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep},
987	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) guarantees that these ambient
988	  intersections assemble the homogeneous submodules \(N, N'\), so the data depends
989	  only on the ambient families \(n \mapsto N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\),
990	  \(n \mapsto N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n\).
991	\end{definition}
992	
993	\subsubsection*{Ambient homogeneity calculus}
994	
995	The kernel/cokernel closure and the degreewise difference identity rest on a small
996	calculus of how a degree-raising endomorphism interacts with the internal grading and
997	with the lattice of homogeneous submodules. Throughout, \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\)
998	is the fixed internally graded \(\kappa\)-module and \(x : M \to M\) is a
999	\(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism raising degree by one. None of these statements forms a
1000	derived carrier: every object is a submodule of the fixed \(M\).
1001	
1002	\begin{definition}\leanok
1003	[Degree-raising endomorphism]
1004	  \label{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1005	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.RaisesDegree}
1006	  Let \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) be an internally graded \(\kappa\)-module. A
1007	  \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism \(x : M \to M\) \emph{raises degree by one} when it
1008	  carries each graded piece into the next: \(x(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq
1009	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) for every \(n\). This is the grading-ring-free form of
1010	  ``multiplication by a degree-one element'': it abstracts each of the \(r\)
1011	  commuting degree-one generators \(x \in R_1\) acting on \(M\) into a single
1012	  endomorphism, with no reference to the grading ring \(R\).
1013	\end{definition}
1014	
1015	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1016	[Membership form of degree-raising]
1017	  \label{lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1018	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.RaisesDegree.mem}
1019	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1020	  If \(x\) raises degree by one (\cref{def:graded_raisesDegree}) and \(m \in
1021	  \mathcal{M}_n\), then \(x m \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\).
1022	\end{lemma}
1023	
1024	\begin{proof}
1025	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1026	  Immediate from the definition: \(x m \in x(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq
1027	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\).
1028	\end{proof}
1029	
1030	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1031	[Degree-shift commutation]
1032	  \label{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1033	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.decompose_raisesDegree}
1034	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree, lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1035	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one. Then for every \(m \in M\) and every \(i\) the
1036	  degree-\((i+1)\) homogeneous component of \(x m\) equals \(x\) applied to the
1037	  degree-\(i\) component of \(m\):
1038	  \[
1039	    \mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m) \;=\; x\bigl(\mathrm{proj}_i(m)\bigr) .
1040	  \]
1041	  This load-bearing commutation says a degree-raising endomorphism shifts the
1042	  internal decomposition by exactly one slot; it is what makes preimages and images
1043	  of homogeneous submodules under \(x\) homogeneous.
1044	\end{lemma}
1045	
1046	\begin{proof}
1047	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree, lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1048	  Decompose \(m = \sum_j \mathrm{proj}_j(m)\) into its finitely many homogeneous
1049	  components and apply \(x\): \(x m = \sum_j x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m))\) with
1050	  \(x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m)) \in \mathcal{M}_{j+1}\) by
1051	  \cref{lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}. Reading off the degree-\((i+1)\) component, all
1052	  terms with \(j+1 \ne i+1\) drop out, leaving \(x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m))\).
1053	\end{proof}
1054	
1055	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1056	[Degree-raising kills the bottom]
1057	  \label{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1058	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1059	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1060	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one. Then the degree-\(0\) component of \(x m\) vanishes
1061	  for every \(m\): \(\mathrm{proj}_0(x m) = 0\).
1062	\end{lemma}
1063	
1064	\begin{proof}
1065	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1066	  Writing \(x m = \sum_j x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m))\) with each summand in
1067	  \(\mathcal{M}_{j+1}\), no summand has degree \(0\) (every \(j+1 \ge 1\)), so the
1068	  degree-\(0\) component is \(0\).
1069	\end{proof}
1070	
1071	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1072	[Preimage of a homogeneous submodule is homogeneous]
1073	  \label{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}
1074	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.comap_isHomogeneous}
1075	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1076	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N' \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
1077	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then the preimage \(x^{-1}(N') =
1078	  \{m : x m \in N'\}\) is a homogeneous submodule of \(M\).
1079	\end{lemma}
1080	
1081	\begin{proof}
1082	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1083	  Let \(m \in x^{-1}(N')\) and fix a degree \(i\). By the degree-shift commutation
1084	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}), \(x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m)) =
1085	  \mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m)\), which lies in \(N'\) because \(x m \in N'\) and \(N'\)
1086	  is homogeneous. Hence \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in x^{-1}(N')\), so \(x^{-1}(N')\) is
1087	  homogeneous.
1088	\end{proof}
1089	
1090	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1091	[Image of a homogeneous submodule is homogeneous]
1092	  \label{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1093	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_isHomogeneous}
1094	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree,
1095	    lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1096	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule.
1097	  Then the image \(x \cdot N = \{x m : m \in N\}\) is a homogeneous submodule of
1098	  \(M\), with degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(x(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) and zero
1099	  degree-\(0\) piece.
1100	\end{lemma}
1101	
1102	\begin{proof}
1103	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree,
1104	    lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1105	  Take \(x m \in x \cdot N\) with \(m \in N\) and fix a degree. In degree \(0\) the
1106	  component \(\mathrm{proj}_0(x m) = 0 \in x \cdot N\)
1107	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}). In degree \(i+1\),
1108	  \(\mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m) = x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m))\)
1109	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}); since \(N\) is homogeneous,
1110	  \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in N\), so this component lies in \(x \cdot N\). Thus every
1111	  homogeneous component of \(x m\) lies in \(x \cdot N\), which is therefore
1112	  homogeneous.
1113	\end{proof}
1114	
1115	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1116	[Intersection of homogeneous submodules is homogeneous]
1117	  \label{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}
1118	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.inf_isHomogeneous}
1119	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1120	  If \(p, q \subseteq M\) are homogeneous submodules
1121	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}) then so is their intersection \(p
1122	  \cap q\). Mathlib provides no lattice-closure lemmas for homogeneous submodules, so
1123	  this is recorded here.
1124	\end{lemma}
1125	
1126	\begin{proof}
1127	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1128	  For \(m \in p \cap q\) and any degree \(i\), homogeneity of \(p\) and of \(q\)
1129	  gives \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in p\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in q\), hence
1130	  \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in p \cap q\).
1131	\end{proof}
1132	
1133	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1134	[Sum of homogeneous submodules is homogeneous]
1135	  \label{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1136	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.sup_isHomogeneous}
1137	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1138	  If \(p, q \subseteq M\) are homogeneous submodules then so is their sum \(p + q\).
1139	\end{lemma}
1140	
1141	\begin{proof}
1142	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1143	  Write an element of \(p + q\) as \(a + b\) with \(a \in p\), \(b \in q\). For any
1144	  degree \(i\), \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a+b) = \mathrm{proj}_i(a) + \mathrm{proj}_i(b)\)
1145	  with \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a) \in p\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_i(b) \in q\) by homogeneity,
1146	  so \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a+b) \in p + q\).
1147	\end{proof}
1148	
1149	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1150	[Ambient image identity]
1151	  \label{lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq}
1152	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_inf_degree_eq}
1153	  \uses{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1154	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N \subseteq M\) be homogeneous. Then the
1155	  degree-\((n+1)\) part of the image \(x \cdot N\) is exactly \(x\) of the
1156	  degree-\(n\) part of \(N\):
1157	  \[
1158	    (x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1} \;=\; x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n) .
1159	  \]
1160	\end{lemma}
1161	
1162	\begin{proof}
1163	  \uses{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1164	  \(\supseteq\): for \(m \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), \(x m \in x \cdot N\) and \(x m
1165	  \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) since \(x\) raises degree. \(\subseteq\): an element \(x m
1166	  \in x \cdot N\) lying in \(\mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) equals its own degree-\((n+1)\)
1167	  component \(\mathrm{proj}_{n+1}(x m) = x(\mathrm{proj}_n(m))\)
1168	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}); homogeneity of \(N\) puts
1169	  \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), so \(x m \in x \cdot (N \cap
1170	  \mathcal{M}_n)\). The image \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous by
1171	  \cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}.
1172	\end{proof}
1173	
1174	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1175	[Ambient distributive law]
1176	  \label{lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1177	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.sup_inf_degree_eq}
1178	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous,
1179	    lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1180	  For homogeneous submodules \(P, Q \subseteq M\) and any degree \(k\), intersecting
1181	  the sum with a graded piece distributes:
1182	  \[
1183	    (P + Q) \cap \mathcal{M}_k \;=\; (P \cap \mathcal{M}_k) + (Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k) .
1184	  \]
1185	\end{lemma}
1186	
1187	\begin{proof}
1188	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous,
1189	    lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1190	  \(\supseteq\) is the general lattice inequality. For \(\subseteq\), take \(z = p +
1191	  q \in (P + Q) \cap \mathcal{M}_k\) with \(p \in P\), \(q \in Q\). Since \(z \in
1192	  \mathcal{M}_k\), \(z = \mathrm{proj}_k(z) = \mathrm{proj}_k(p) +
1193	  \mathrm{proj}_k(q)\); homogeneity of \(P\) and \(Q\)
1194	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}) puts \(\mathrm{proj}_k(p) \in P \cap
1195	  \mathcal{M}_k\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_k(q) \in Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k\), so \(z \in (P
1196	  \cap \mathcal{M}_k) + (Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k)\). The intersection and sum stay in
1197	  the homogeneous lattice by \cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous} and
1198	  \cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}.
1199	\end{proof}

1201	\subsubsection*{Closure under kernel and cokernel}
1202	
1203	\begin{lemma}[Kernel/cokernel closure of the subquotient class]
1204	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}
1205	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_ker_coker}
1206	  % NOTE: (iter-018) this \lean pin names a declaration that does NOT exist in Lean —
1207	  % the content is split across 8 component lemmas (ker_isHomogeneous, coker_isHomogeneous,
1208	  % ker_le, coker_le, ker_annihilate, coker_annihilate, comap_map_le_of_commute,
1209	  % map_map_le_of_commute) plus the SubquotientDatum.ker/.coker constructors (not yet built).
1210	  % Planner: either add a bundled subquotient_ker_coker wrapper or re-pin to the 8 components.
1211	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1212	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1213	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1214	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1215	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1216	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
1217	  endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\), and set \(x = t_{r-1}\). Form the two new
1218	  pairs of homogeneous submodules of \(M\)
1219	  \[
1220	    K \;=\; \bigl(\,N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\ N'\,\bigr),
1221	    \qquad
1222	    C \;=\; \bigl(\,N,\ N' + x \cdot N\,\bigr),
1223	  \]
1224	  where \(x^{-1}(N') = \{\,m \in M : x \cdot m \in N'\,\}\) is the preimage and
1225	  \(x \cdot N\) is the image of \(N\) under \(x\). Then:
1226	  \begin{enumerate}
1227	    \item \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N\) are homogeneous submodules of
1228	      \(M\), with \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\), so both
1229	      \(K\) and \(C\) are subquotient pairs;
1230	    \item the remaining endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\) pairwise commute,
1231	      raise degree by one, and preserve both members of \(K\) and of \(C\); and
1232	    \item \(x\) annihilates both subquotients: \(x \cdot (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \subseteq N'\)
1233	      and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\).
1234	  \end{enumerate}
1235	  Consequently \(K\) and \(C\) are again subquotient data, now of length \(r-1\)
1236	  (the endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\)), and \(K = \ker x\) and \(C =
1237	  \operatorname{coker} x\) on the subquotient \(N/N'\): \(K\) represents
1238	  \(\ker\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\) and \(C\) represents
1239	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\). No quotient or subtype
1240	  carrier is constructed; \(K\) and \(C\) are ambient pairs of homogeneous
1241	  submodules of the fixed \(M\).
1242	\end{lemma}
1243	
1244	\begin{proof}
1245	  \uses{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1246	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1247	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1248	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1249	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1250	  \emph{Homogeneity.} The preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous: if
1251	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(x \cdot m \in N'\), then since \(x\) raises
1252	  degree by one (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) the components
1253	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) lie in distinct degrees, and
1254	  homogeneity of \(N'\) (split \(G_1\),
1255	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) forces each
1256	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in N'\), i.e.\ \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in x^{-1}(N')\).
1257	  Thus \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is an intersection of homogeneous submodules, hence
1258	  homogeneous. The image \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous with degree-\((n+1)\) piece
1259	  \(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) by
1260	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}, and a sum of homogeneous
1261	  submodules is homogeneous (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib} applied at the
1262	  module level), so \(N' + x \cdot N\) is homogeneous. The inclusions
1263	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (as \(x \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)) and
1264	  \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\) (as \(N' \le N\) and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N\)) are
1265	  immediate. In the language of the ambient homogeneity calculus, this paragraph is
1266	  exactly: \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous (\cref{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}),
1267	  \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous (\cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}), and the
1268	  homogeneous lattice is closed under intersection
1269	  (\cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}) and sum
1270	  (\cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}).
1271	
1272	  \emph{Preservation.} Each \(t_i\) with \(i \le r-2\) commutes with \(x\) and
1273	  preserves \(N, N'\); hence it preserves \(x^{-1}(N')\) (if \(x t_i m = t_i x m \in
1274	  N'\) whenever \(x m \in N'\)), so it preserves \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\), and it
1275	  preserves \(x \cdot N\) (as \(t_i x N = x t_i N \subseteq x N\)), so it preserves
1276	  \(N' + x \cdot N\). Degree-raising and commutativity are inherited.
1277	
1278	  \emph{Annihilation by \(x\).} For \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) one has
1279	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\) by definition, so \(x\) carries the first member of \(K\) into
1280	  its second; and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\) trivially. Thus \(x\) acts as
1281	  zero on \(N/N'\) restricted to \(K\) and on the cokernel quotient \(C\).
1282	
1283	  \emph{Identification with \(\ker x\) and \(\operatorname{coker} x\).} On the
1284	  subquotient \(N/N'\), the class of \(m \in N\) is killed by \(x\) iff
1285	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\), i.e.\ iff \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\); so \(\ker(x : N/N' \to

1288	  \((N/N')/\operatorname{im} x = N/(N' + x \cdot N)\) is represented by \(C\).

1380	
1381	\begin{lemma}[Finiteness transfer for the subquotients]
1382	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}
1383	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_finite_transfer}
1384	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1385	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}
1386	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum of length \(r \ge 1\)
1387	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}), so that \(M\) is finite over
1388	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), and let \(x = t_{r-1}\). Then the two
1389	  subquotients
1390	  \[
1391	    K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N'), \qquad C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)
1392	  \]
1393	  of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker} are finite modules over
1394	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\); that is, they are subquotient data of length
1395	  \(r-1\), satisfying the finiteness condition. This is what is needed for the
1396	  inductive hypothesis of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}.
1397	
1398	  The kernel \(K\) and cokernel \(C\) of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}
1399	  satisfy the same conditions as the original datum -- a homogeneous pair, pairwise-commuting
1400	  degree-raising endomorphisms preserving the pair, a polynomial-ring module structure, and
1401	  the finiteness condition -- but of length \(r-1\) rather than \(r\).
1402	\end{lemma}
1403	
1404	\begin{proof}
1405	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1406	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}
1407	  \emph{The polynomial module structure on \(M\).} The \(r\) pairwise-commuting
1408	  degree-raising endomorphisms \(x_0, \dots, x_{r-1}\) generate a \(\kappa\)-subalgebra
1409	  \(A = \mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{x_0, \dots, x_{r-1}\}\) of the \emph{noncommutative}
1410	  endomorphism algebra \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\); because the generators
1411	  commute pairwise, \(A\) is commutative (\cref{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}).
1412	  Commutativity of \(A\) is exactly what makes the evaluation homomorphism at the
1413	  generators a legal \(\kappa\)-algebra homomorphism \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]
1414	  \to A\): evaluation refuses the noncommutative \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) as a
1415	  target. Composing with the inclusion \(A \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\)
1416	  and restricting scalars along it endows \(M\) with the structure of a module over
1417	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), compatible with the \(\kappa\)-action and
1418	  characterised by \(t_i \cdot m = x_i m\).
1419	
1420	  \emph{Transfer down one generator.} Set \(A = \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) and
1421	  \(x = t_{r-1}\). Since \(M\) is finite over the Noetherian ring \(A\), \(M\) is a
1422	  Noetherian \(A\)-module, so the submodule \(N \cap x^{-1}(N') \subseteq M\) is
1423	  finite over \(A\) and the quotients \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'))/N'\) and
1424	  \(N/(N' + x \cdot N)\) are finite over \(A\). By
1425	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker} the element \(x = t_{r-1}\) annihilates
1426	  both subquotients, so the \(A\)-action on each factors through the surjection of
1427	  polynomial rings
1428	  \[
1429	    A = \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]
1430	  \]
1431	  fixing \(t_i\) for \(i < r-1\) and sending \(t_{r-1} \mapsto 0\). Applying the
1432	  finite-generation transfer along a surjection
1433	  (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}) turns finiteness over \(A\)
1434	  into finiteness over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\), supplying the length-\((r-1)\)
1435	  finiteness witnesses for \(K\) and \(C\).
1436	
1437	  \emph{Why the free polynomial ring.} The smaller ring must be the \emph{free}
1438	  polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\), not the subalgebra
1439	  \(\mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{x_0, \dots, x_{r-2}\} \subseteq
1440	  \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\): relations among the endomorphisms inside
1441	  \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) would obstruct the polynomial-ring surjection that
1442	  the scalar transfer requires. Keeping free polynomial rings at every step keeps the
1443	  surjection -- and hence the transfer -- available throughout. Everything is finite
1444	  generation of ambient submodules of the fixed \(M\) and their quotients.
1445	\end{proof}

1569	\section{Support and freeness predicates}
1570	\label{sec:quot_predicates}
1571	
1572	The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
1573	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
1574	geometric conditions: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support
1575	proper over the base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally
1576	free of a fixed rank}. This section introduces both predicates.
1577	
1578	\begin{definition}

1586	  % \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U) \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\)
1587	  % (\cref{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}) is closed. The reverse inclusion, which identifies
1588	  % the annihilator sheaf with the module-annihilator on affine opens, remains bridge-gated on
1589	  % \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}.
1590	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
1591	  % Polynomials" (read from
1592	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
1593	  % SOURCE QUOTE:

1596	  %  be any coherent sheaf on

1600	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is \emph{quasi-coherent and of finite type}
1601	  (over a locally noetherian base, so that on each affine open \(U\) the section
1602	  module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is a finitely generated \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module).
1603	  The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ideal sheaf
1604	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) defined on affine opens: for each affine open
1605	  \(U \subseteq X\),

1609	    \;\subseteq\; \mathcal{O}_X(U),

1612	  local data patch to a coherent ideal sheaf on \(X\) whose vanishing locus is

1663	[Annihilator of a finitely generated module commutes with
1664	  localization]
1665	  \label{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map}
1666	  \lean{Module.annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map}
1667	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(S \subseteq R\) be a multiplicative
1668	  submonoid, and let \(R_S = S^{-1}R\) be the localization of \(R\) at \(S\). Let
1669	  \(M\) be a \emph{finitely generated} \(R\)-module and let \(f : M \to M_S\) be
1670	  an \(R\)-linear map exhibiting \(M_S\) as the localized module \(S^{-1}M\)
1671	  (i.e.\ \(f\) is a localization of \(M\) at \(S\)). Then the annihilator of
1672	  \(M_S\) over \(R_S\) is the extension of the annihilator of \(M\) along the
1673	  structure map \(R \to R_S\):
1674	  \[
1675	    \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)
1676	    \;=\; (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S
1677	    \;=\; \mathrm{map}\bigl(R \to R_S\bigr)\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}_R M\bigr).
1678	  \]
1679	  Equivalently, for finitely generated \(M\) the annihilator commutes with
1680	  localization: \(\mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M) = S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M)\).

1683	  finitely generated the inclusion \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M) \subseteq
1684	  \mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M)\) can be strict, so the identity fails. This is the
1685	  standard commutative-algebra fact that the annihilator of a finite module
1686	  localizes; it is recorded here as the load-bearing algebra engine for the
1687	  basic-open coherence of \cref{def:modules_annihilator}.

1103

1105	variable (ℳ : ℕ → Submodule κ M) [DirectSum.Decomposition ℳ]

1110	over the free polynomial ring `MvPolynomial (Fin r) κ` acting through the `t i`; the carriers
1111	involved are the ambient `t`-stable submodules `polySubmodule … N`, `polySubmodule … N'`. -/
1112	structure SubquotientDatum (r : ℕ) where
1113	  /-- The upper homogeneous submodule. -/
1114	  N : Submodule κ M
1115	  /-- The lower homogeneous submodule. -/
1116	  N' : Submodule κ M
1117	  /-- `N'` is contained in `N`. -/
1118	  hle : N' ≤ N
1119	  /-- `N` is homogeneous. -/
1120	  hN : N.IsHomogeneous ℳ
1121	  /-- `N'` is homogeneous. -/
1122	  hN' : N'.IsHomogeneous ℳ

1124	  t : Fin r → Module.End κ M

1126	  hcomm : ∀ i j, Commute (t i) (t j)

1128	  hraise : ∀ i, RaisesDegree ℳ (t i)
1129	  /-- Each preserves `N`. -/

1132	  hpresN' : ∀ i, N'.map (t i) ≤ N'
